content:
Substrate type and aging method exert statistically significant effects on bond strength, while the choice of adhesive approach does not. Three-way ANOVA demonstrated that substrate (p = 0.00) and aging method (p = 0.00) were significant factors, whereas adhesive approach had no significant influence (p = 0.84) [REF:8]. This finding reframes the failure mode question: degradation outcomes depend more on the interface being stressed and the environmental protocol applied than on the adhesive formulation itself [REF:8]. The resistance of resin-dentin bonds to thermal-aging degradation was material dependent [REF:11], and among the adhesive systems tested, one-step self-etch adhesives obtained the lowest microtensile bond strength (MTBS) results after both aging treatments, with significantly reduced adhesive capacity following thermocycling [REF:11]. Not all adhesive chemistries, however, succumb to aging: the experimental non-methacrylate adhesive EA 5 experienced no changes (p > 0.05) in bonding performance following the entire 1-year duration of biochemical aging [REF:4].

| Factor | p-value | Significance | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Substrate | 0.00 | Significant | [REF:8] |
| Aging method | 0.00 | Significant | [REF:8] |
| Adhesive approach | 0.84 | Not significant | [REF:8] |

The chemical degradation pathway in EVA encapsulants under damp heat proceeds through decomposition into acetic acid, which in turn reacts with perovskite layers and causes yellow PbI2 degradation [REF:12]. This acetic acid release represents a failure mechanism specific to EVA chemistry and distinguishes it from non-acetate-containing encapsulants. At the interfacial level, EVA at the glass interface degrades more than at the cell interface, explaining why this initially stronger interface becomes the preferential site for delamination [REF:14]. The differential degradation across the two EVA interfaces [REF:14] aligns with the ANOVA finding that substrate type drives bond strength outcomes [REF:8], as the glass and cell surfaces present fundamentally different chemical environments for moisture interaction and adhesive breakdown.

Material properties beyond chemistry further constrain encapsulant durability. PEVA18 showed inadequate transmittance and relatively high stiffness after 2000 h of damp-heat aging, discouraging its use as an encapsulant [REF:9]. Elevated stiffness after aging increases the mechanical stress at the interface during thermal cycling, potentially lowering the delamination threshold. This stiffness-mediated pathway operates alongside the chemical degradation routes documented for EVA [REF:12][REF:14] and represents an independent contributor to failure mode transitions in polyethylene-vinyl acetate variants [REF:9].

The long-term trajectory of adhesion loss provides a bridge between accelerated laboratory aging and field performance. At 10,000 hours of accelerated aging, EVA adhesion falls to a level generally seen after long-term field exposures of more than 15 years [REF:13]. The EVA manufacturer STR has suggested a minimum of 53 N/cm peel strength after lamination (measured by 180° peel) as sufficient to ensure a 20-year lifetime [REF:10]. These two data points—the 10,000-hour laboratory equivalence to >15 years of field exposure [REF:13] and the 53 N/cm durability threshold [REF:10]—together frame the practical boundary conditions. When accelerated aging drives peel strength below this manufacturer-specified floor, the encapsulant system transitions from a regime of gradual interfacial weakening to one where delamination becomes the dominant failure mode.

Several methodological limitations constrain these findings. The statistical independence of adhesive approach from bond strength [REF:8] was demonstrated in a specific substrate-adhesive matrix and may not generalize to all encapsulant-substrate combinations encountered in flexible electronics. The EA 5 result showing zero degradation over one year of biochemical aging [REF:4] applies to a non-methacrylate formulation that differs fundamentally from the EVA and POE systems central to photovoltaic encapsulation. The EVA deacetylation pathway producing acetic acid [REF:12] is chemistry-specific and does not extend to polyolefin or thermoplastic encapsulants. Quantitative fracture energy measurements that would enable direct comparison of failure mode transitions across encapsulant families remain sparse in the available evidence.

**Key Findings**

- Substrate type and aging method significantly affect bond strength (p = 0.00 for both), while adhesive approach does not (p = 0.84), indicating that interfacial conditions dominate over adhesive formulation [REF:8].
- EVA decomposes into acetic acid under damp heat, reacting with perovskite and causing PbI2 degradation, a failure mechanism specific to acetate-containing encapsulants [REF:12].
- EVA at the glass interface degrades more than at the cell interface, making the initially stronger glass interface the preferential delamination site [REF:14].
- At 10,000 hours of accelerated aging, EVA adhesion reaches levels equivalent to >15 years of field exposure, against a manufacturer-recommended minimum of 53 N/cm for 20-year lifetime [REF:13][REF:10].
- The experimental non-methacrylate adhesive EA 5 showed no degradation (p > 0.05) over 1 year of biochemical aging, suggesting alternative chemistries may resist aging-induced failure modes [REF:4].
